1.text: The module docstring establishes the project identity. Naming it ttok4bedrock signals that this is a Bedrock-native alternative to Simon Willison's ttok library, which uses OpenAI's tokenizer.

6.text: These imports (boto3, ClientError, json) are unused in this file — they exist so that import ttok4bedrock makes them available in the package namespace. This is a common Python pattern for convenience re-exports, though it slightly increases import time.

12.text: __all__ defines the public API contract: only count_tokens and truncate. This matches the original ttok interface exactly, so users can do import ttok4bedrock as ttok and their existing code works unchanged.

15.text: The count_tokens function is the primary SDK entry point. Its signature mirrors ttok.count_tokens() — same parameter names, same return type — making migration a one-line import change.

17.text: The default model is the latest Claude Sonnet. This means users get a working setup with zero configuration beyond AWS credentials — an important UX choice for a drop-in replacement.

45.text: The import is deferred to function call time rather than module load time. This avoids circular imports since bedrock_counter.py is in the same package, and means import ttok4bedrock doesn't trigger boto3 client creation.

46.text: A fresh BedrockTokenCounter is created on every call. This is a deliberate simplicity trade-off: the SDK API is stateless and thread-safe, but the LRU cache is not shared across calls. For repeated use, import BedrockTokenCounter directly.

50.text: The truncate function provides the second half of the ttok-compatible API. It uses the same adaptive algorithm as the CLI's -t flag.

82.text: Same deferred import pattern as count_tokens. Both functions are thin wrappers — the real logic lives entirely in BedrockTokenCounter.
